data.title: Yancoal: Likely Acquisition Of Premium Metallurgical Coal Assets Makes It Attractive
data.description:
I covered Yancoal (OTCPK:YACAF or YAL for its ASX primary listing) previously, and in my most recent review, I suggested waiting for a pullback before adding shares or initiating a long position. Since such a pullback occurred and because the company recently released its report for the July-September period, it is worth wondering if Yancoal is now attractive enough to load up.

Nothing surprising in Q3 2025

Rainfalls continued to disturb mining operations in Q3 2025 so that Yancoal reported a slight fall in attributable raw coal production over the period, despite having increased its stake in the Moolarben coal mine by 3.75% in the previous quarter. Hence, Yancoal produced 11.6 million metric tons of coal in the July-September period, with about 85% of it being thermal coal. While the total attributable production volume represents a 9% drop compared to Q2, it is important to remember here that year-to-date production is still 2% above last year’s. I would not read too much into this small quarterly drop, simply because mining output is volatile by nature. The fact that Yancoal kept its full-year production guidance unchanged is reassuring.

When it comes to coal sales, the quarter-on-quarter drop was even smaller than for production, as the company was finally able to deliver some of the cargoes that were promised to clients in the previous quarter but had remained stuck at Newcastle port. The situation at the port’s two export terminals has slightly improved in Q3, even if the vessel queue at PWCS coal terminal, the largest of the two facilities, remains well above its long-term average of about 20 ships (Figure 1).

The reported average sales price for coal cargoes (all types) came out at A$140 per metric ton and was almost unchanged compared to Q2. This is explained by the fact that Australian coal indexes have been relatively flat over the past few months. Meanwhile, Yancoal was able to keep its average production cost in Q3 just below its annual target of A$93 per metric ton, highlighting stable business operations.

The only real event for the quarter was the change of CEO. In early September, Sharif Burra replaced Ning Yue. This new CEO is highly experienced with mining asset valuation for both open-cut and underground facilities. To me, this constitutes another clear sign that Yancoal is stepping up its efforts to acquire some high-quality metallurgical coal assets at a time when valuations have come down significantly amidst weakening coal prices and also when some top-quality mines in Australia are either officially for sale (e.g., Anglo American’s assets) or the object of strong rumors regarding a possible sale (e.g. BHP’s assets).

Regarding the outlook for thermal coal prices, Yancoal is way more optimistic than its Indonesian counterparts, even if the company notices that market conditions continue to be “challenging,” as “there is still excess coal supply in many regions.” The major issue of 2025 for thermal coal exporters has so far been the significant drop in Chinese imports. In Australia, this makes producers of 5,500 kcal/kg coal grade like Yancoal particularly exposed to this slowdown, as Chinese consumers prefer this grade to 6,000 kcal/kg coal cargoes mostly exported to Japan, South Korea, and Taiwan. Yancoal underlines that despite weak prices, “supply remains stubbornly consistent.” This is explained by the fact that coal miners generated so much cash in the 2022-2023 period that they have been able to withstand unfavorable market conditions for way longer than usual. Having said that, lower-grade coal producers in Indonesia, the direct competitors of Yancoal in the Chinese market, have started to reduce output (Figure 2). This suggests that the worst may actually already be behind us, but projections are never easy with commodities, and many analysts, including those at Argus, continue to hold a bearish outlook for both thermal and metallurgical coal for 2026.

Last month, a dispute involving coal miner Glencore and major Asian consumer Taipower broke out. While Yancoal is not part of this dispute, many traders have been speculating about the probability to see the company benefit from it.

The ongoing dispute between Glencore and Taipower: an opportunity for Yancoal?

A few weeks before Yancoal released its Q3 report, Taipower, the state-owned power producer in Taiwan, announced the ban of Australian thermal coal cargoes supplied by Glencore, the giant commodity trading firm headquartered in Switzerland. Seeing that some of the banned coal cargoes can easily be replaced by Yancoal, a simple question arose: could this ban represent an opportunity for Yancoal?

Taipower is still a large consumer of thermal coal in Asia, even if the country is slowly moving toward greener solutions. In 2024, about 80% of all the electricity produced by the power utility company, which itself accounted for about 70% of all the power produced on the island, was still coming from coal. In the calendar year of 2025, Taipower is expected to consume around 21 million metric tons. While the company does not publish the amount of coal purchased per country of origin, official import data show that Taiwan imported about 35.5 million metric tons of coal in January-August 2025, with 45% of this total coming from Australia. If we apply this percentage to Taipower, this means that the company should consume about 9-10 million tons of Australian thermal coal this year.

Taipower is purchasing coal through both long-term contracts, for about 80% of the volume consumed each year, and spot transactions. Five of these long-term supply contracts for Australian thermal coal were signed with Glencore. In early October, Taipower suspended these contracts. The legal dispute is ongoing, and I do not know who will prevail, but what looks obvious is the fact that Taipower needs to replace these Glencore cargoes, and Yancoal may naturally come out on top of the list of potential winners here. Indeed, three of these five suspended contracts are related to coal produced from Hunter Valley Operations (HVO). This coal mining complex is operated by Yancoal but co-owned by Yancoal and Glencore (51-49%, respectively).

While Yancoal is above all a supplier of 5,500 kcal/kg coal grade, it also produces, at HVO, one of the best-quality thermal coals you can find across Australia. Assuming that Taipower is looking for a product whose specifications are close to the cargoes supplied by Glencore (i.e., high calorific value, low ash, and low sulfur), replacing HVO coal may be tricky for Taipower simply because there are not many alternatives available.

While Taipower has banned Glencore HVO cargoes, Yancoal HVO cargoes could constitute a logical alternative. Having said that, this is not as easy as it may look. Indeed, boosting HVO mine production seems out of the question, as the mine is already close to its maximum authorized annual run-of-mine production of 42 million tons. If Yancoal cannot increase output at the mine, there are only two ways to make Yancoal HVO coal available to Taipower: through client switching with Glencore or by becoming a middleman between Glencore and Taipower. I, personally, believe that Taipower would not accept any of these solutions, at least until the current legal dispute is ongoing.

Consequently, while I understand why many see an opportunity for Yancoal here, at least on paper, I am more than skeptical and do not expect this dispute to have a strong impact on Yancoal. Obviously, for a company like Yancoal selling about 40 million tons of coal annually, a boost of several million tons in sales annually could give a significant boost to the company’s bottom line, but I do not believe that this switch from HVO Glencore to HVO Yancoal will occur and rather think that Taipower may try to boost purchases from Whitehaven’s Maules Creek, for instance, a top-tier mine with some spare production capacity left. Taipower could also potentially resume purchasing cheap bituminous coal cargoes from Russia, for which imports have been stopped since H2 2022.

Valuation: still not particularly cheap

When you report the volume of coal sold to gross profit in Q3, the reported profit was actually relatively weak (Figure 3). Indeed, since 2023, each time Yancoal was able to produce 10 million tons of coal in a single quarter, the associated profit was generally higher than A$800 million. In Q3, this number barely broke above A$500 million!

Yancoal's share price has been falling in recent weeks, but not enough to make it cheap. Yancoal’s valuation to enterprise value ratio, which basically compares the value given by investors to the value given by accountants (Figure 4), has been above its 6-year average since early 2022, and I would personally like to see this value come closer to 1, even potentially fall slightly below it, before labeling the company as cheap. Let me cautiously remind you here that the last time this ratio pushed above 1.4, in Q1 2023, the share price fell by more than 30% over the following 6 months!

A similar conclusion can be drawn when looking at the capitalization to gross profit ratio (Figure 5). This ratio measures the value given by investors to Yancoal in relation to the gross profit reported. This ratio may be dangerous to use when commodity prices skyrocket because, in such a case, abnormally high sales prices tend to artificially boost profits. Where global coal prices currently stand, this risk clearly does not exist so that this ratio can be considered as meaningful. Again, the value displayed by this ratio does not support in any way the idea that Yancoal is currently undervalued.

It is often highlighted that Yancoal looks cheap when compared to its peers, and particularly to its most direct competitor, Whitehaven Coal (OTCPK:WHITF). Indeed, when you compare these two Aussie coal miners, a paradox quickly occurs: while Yancoal constantly reports higher sales revenue, its valuation has always been lower (Figures 6 and 7).

There are many reasons to explain Yancoal’s lower valuation, but the two most important seem to be the relative weight of its primary shareholder and the average quality of the coal it sells. Indeed, Yancoal is 65% owned by a major Chinese company, Yankuang Energy Group. The fact that a single company can actually control Yancoal makes it uninvestable for many activists and institutional investors. Also, even if Yancoal has high-grade product like HVO coal in its portfolio, this should not hide the fact that the company is above all a 5,500 kcal/kg coal producer and not a 6,000 kcal/kg coal supplier like Whitehaven Coal. Investors tend to value 5,500 kcal/kg coal producers lower because the market for this product is way more volatile than for top-quality thermal coal products. While Yankuang Energy Group is not expected to reduce its stake anytime soon, Yancoal seems, however, ready to significantly upgrade the value of its products through acquisition. Indeed, the miner is actively looking at acquiring top-quality metallurgical coal assets in Queensland.

Currently, Whitehaven produces way more metallurgical coal than Yancoal, and its cargoes, primarily made of hard coking coal and semi-hard coking coal, are of higher value. Indeed, by comparison, Yancoal produces about 50% less metallurgical coal and only semi-soft coking coal and PCI grades, which are among the lowest grades for metallurgical coal. The difference in value is significant: for instance, the price difference between mid-quality hard coking coal and high-quality PCI is about US$30 per metric ton (FOB Australia) at the time of writing.

For coal miners like Yancoal, the focus has slowly but surely turned to metallurgical coal. This is simply related to the outlook for each product: while thermal coal demand is expected to slowly but surely slow down over the next decade, demand for metallurgical coal, especially from India and emerging countries in Asia, should actually jump. The fact that lenders are keen to fund metallurgical coal projects is also pushing thermal coal miners to diversify. However, it is worth noting here that the end of thermal coal has been constantly delayed, and the current shortage of gas turbines may keep coal power plants running across Asia for longer than many think.

The high probability of seeing Yancoal become the owner and operator of major premium hard coking coal assets in Australia sometime in 2026 brings us to another paradox: I think that accumulating Yancoal shares currently makes sense, even if valuation is not particularly attractive. As a large producer of hard coking coal, Yancoal would attract more investors while significantly reducing the cost of capital and insurance, leading to increased valuation. The premium valuation associated with metallurgical coal producers against thermal coal producers was, for instance, highlighted by Whitehaven Coal. For Yancoal, the higher the quality of the assets purchased , the higher the jump in valuation should become. Hence, if the company is able to grab some of the premium metallurgical coal assets currently owned by Anglo American or BHP, the jump in valuation should be maximal.

Can Yancoal grab Anglo American’s or BHP’s assets?

About Anglo American assets

Anglo American (OTCQX:NGLOY) was supposed to sell its Australian metallurgical coal assets to Peabody Energy (BTU), but the deal fell apart in last August following a fire incident at the Moranbah North underground coal mine in April 2025. A legal dispute between Anglo American and the US firm is ongoing. In any case, at this stage, it seems unlikely that Peabody will become the owner of these assets, as the settlement will likely come in the form of cash, in my opinion. In other words, Anglo still needs to find a buyer, and Yancoal finds here another chance to acquire these assets, especially the Grosvenor and Moranbah North coal mines, which produce top-quality hard coking coal. Yancoal was indeed among the few bidders that competed with Peabody Energy for these assets.

Does it make sense for Yancoal to grab the same assets that fellow Peabody decided to abandon? As often, the answer is pretty simple and can be summarized into a simple “it depends on the price!” We do not know how much Yancoal offered to Anglo at the time of its failed bid in 2024, but it is likely that Yancoal will have an opportunity here to grab these assets at a lower price than the one agreed upon with Peabody. The fire incident and the fact that Anglo is in a hurry to sell appear to work in favor of Yancoal. Indeed, Anglo wants to get rid of these coal assets in 2026 to focus on copper, iron ore, and its ongoing merger with Teck Resources. It has reaffirmed its wish to sell and has even clearly labeled these assets as “exiting businesses” in its Q3 production report. In short, with Peabody out and without many potential bidders on the horizon, Yancoal will likely be able to grab these high-quality-but-hard-to-manage assets at a reduced price in the coming months.

As a reminder, Peabody offered US$2.32 billion in cash and up to US$1 billion of royalties for four mining complexes that were supposed to produce slightly more than 11 million metric tons in 2026. The closure of Moranbah since Q2 2025 is obviously a problem that needs to be resolved, as the mine accounts for about a third of this expected annual volume, but Yancoal has demonstrated with Moolarben its ability to safely manage large underground facilities, so there are objective reasons to be confident here.

Yancoal has about US$1.2 billion on hand, so even if it were to pay the same price as Peabody, this would mean building a debt of about US$1 billion, which does not seem excessive for a company of this size and the quality of these assets. As a reminder, in 2024 alone, the underlying EBITDA for these assets was US$924 million.

While Anglo American’s assets appear to be a logical target for Yancoal, there are also growing rumors among coal traders according to which Yancoal could eye a much larger prey: BHP’s coal assets. This rumor is not really surprising, as Yancoal was already one of the bidders for BMA's Daunia and Blackwater mines.

About BHP’s assets

It is no secret that BHP has been considering a spinoff of its coal assets, which are consuming cash while providing subpar returns. Indeed, so far in 2025, return on capital (RoC) employed has barely remained above water, while the average RoC for the group was above 20%. For a company like BHP, barely breaking even is clearly not good enough, and it has already pre-announced “difficult decisions ahead.”

While the company has been blaming the new taxation system in Queensland, it should not hide the fact that coal simply looks less and less attractive for BHP. Indeed, coal only accounted for 9.8% of the company’s total revenue in FY25 (July 2024-June 2025) and 2.2% of its underlying EBITDA! As a result, a growing number of analysts believe that BHP may soon be tempted to completely abandon coal.

BHP produces metallurgical coal through a joint venture, BHP Mitsubishi Alliance (BMA), with Mitsubishi Corporation. BHP owns 50% of BMA. Purchasing 50% of BMA would constitute a much larger pill to swallow for Yancoal, as BMA's annual production is generally between 35 and 40 million metric tons. Besides, these assets would likely be valued at a higher multiple than Anglo’s, with Peak Downs being the crown jewel here. As a result, to me, the higher tag price and the more complex transaction make this acquisition by Yancoal slightly less likely but clearly not impossible, especially if Yancoal’s parent company is ready to provide cheap capital, as it did in 2017 when Yancoal purchased HVO from Rio Tinto before selling a 49% stake to Glencore.

Conclusion

Yancoal reported steady operations in Q3 without any surprise. In its current form (i.e., as a miner focused on thermal coal), Yancoal's current valuation is not really attractive. However, if you expect, like me, Yancoal to become a large supplier of premium coking coal sometime in 2026, entering bullish positions at the current level seems justified because if a deal is officially announced, the company valuation should jump significantly, especially if Yancoal is able to grab either Anglo American’s or BHP’s premium metallurgical coal assets.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dycom: Power Solutions Acquisition And Strong End Market Demand Should Drive Upside
data.description:
Executive Summary

I last covered Dycom (DY) in February of this year with a buy recommendation, and the stock has performed well since then, gaining ~77%. Dycom’s future growth prospects look attractive. The company has a strong growth visibility, supported by record backlog, accelerating fiber deployments, BEAD funding programs, and a surge in hyperscaler and data center related network buildouts.

The upcoming acquisition of Power Solutions also expands Dycom’s target market. The deal puts the company into the data center for the first time, adds a high-margin electrical contracting platform, and creates clear cross-selling potential with existing relationships with large-scale service providers. The deal valuation also looks attractive at approximately 9.7x EBITDA, or 8.5x after-tax depreciation benefits, especially considering Power Solutions’ mid- to high-teens average margins, strong cash conversion, and ~15% revenue compound annual growth rate - CAGR.

On valuation, the stock is trading at 25.59x FY27 (ending Jan) consensus EPS estimates, which is below its five-year average. I find the valuation reasonable given the strong demand drivers and improving margins. Therefore, I continue to maintain my buy recommendation for this stock.

Recent Financial Performance

Dycom announced good Q3 results, with revenues increasing 14.1% Y/Y, reaching $1.45 bn. Organic growth was ~7.2% Y/Y. The strength was broad-based, with FTTH activity remaining very strong, wireless programs continuing to contribute, and fiber-infrastructure work for hyperscalers remaining solid. Maintenance and operations also performed well.

Margins were also very strong. Adjusted EBITDA margins were ~15.1%, up 169 bps from last year’s 13.4%. Strong execution in the field, operational discipline, efficiency improvements, and ongoing benefits of operating leverage as revenue scale helped margins.

Organic Growth Outlook

Going forward, Dycom’s revenues should benefit from a healthy order backlog and strong end-market demand. The company ended the latest quarter with a $8.22 billion backlog, up 4.7% year over year and about 3% sequentially. This figure doesn’t include $500 million of service and maintenance contracts signed after quarter-end. If you include this amount, the backlog performance is even more positive. The order backlog growth was broad-based with strength in telecommunications, wireless modernization, hyperscalers, and infrastructure.

I expect the end markets to continue to remain strong. Fiber-to-the-home deployments remain one of the biggest near-term growth drivers. Carriers are still focused on building out high-capacity broadband, and on its last earnings call, Dycom said FTTH activity is at a “fever pitch” and should accelerate again next year. So, this is a good medium-term growth driver.

On top of that, BEAD funding deployment is currently underway. The NTIA has now approved final implementation plans for 15 states and three territories. The total cost of the program is expected to be roughly $29.5 billion, with $26 billion for fiber and HFC infrastructure. In its most recent earnings call, management noted that Dycom has already received more than $500 million in verbal orders related to BEAD work, but this amount has not been reflected in the backlog yet. Management expects BEAD-related revenue to begin in Q2 FY27 and then gradually increase throughout the year and beyond. Given the multi-year deployment cycles, BEAD should become a stable and meaningful source of growth over the medium term (4+ years).

Hyperscaler demand is another growth driver. The surge in AI-driven computing is creating a huge demand from hyperscalers and other large data-driven technology companies for new and enhanced fiber networks to connect data centers and interconnects within data center campuses nationwide. Therefore, there is a significant ramp-up potential in outside-plant data center network construction. This is completely separate from any benefits associated with the recent acquisition of Power Solutions, which operates within Data centres and is another major growth driver. With hyperscalers growing their footprints in many regions, I expect Dycom’s data center-related fiber operations to scale meaningfully over the next few years.

Wireless is also holding up well, thanks to increasing 5G density and the ongoing device cycles. Management said current wireless buildout programs will last through 2027, which gives the company a stable mid-term runway in this segment.

All of this sets the stage for solid organic revenue growth for Dycom. The margin outlook is also positive. The company should continue to benefit from operating leverage as revenue grows. Dycom is also implementing a new ERP system, with Phase 1 completed in Q3 and additional phases planned through FY27. That should drive more efficiency and better project execution over time.

Pricing also seems supportive. As telecommunications and hyperscaler work moves toward more complex long-haul and medium-haul deployments with higher-density fiber and installation processes that require more planning, Dycom’s specialized field expertise gives them the power to price affordably. These jobs aren’t commoditized, and customers value performance. Combined with the continued investment in hyperscale technology and the need for complex fiber construction driven by BEAD, the company should be able to increase margins in the medium term.

So, from an organic perspective, the setup looks strong: a record backlog, multiple industry megatrends driving growth, clear visibility for telco and wireless programs through 2027, a multi-year BEAD cycle just getting started, big data center/AI network buildout accelerating in 2026 and beyond, along with good execution and operating leverage helping margins.

Power Solutions Acquisition

On top of the solid organic outlook, Dycom should also receive a major boost from its upcoming acquisition of Power Solutions.

Deal Terms (Source): Dycom will pay a total purchase price of $1.95 billion for Power Solutions. Out of this, approximately $293 million will be paid in Dycom common stock and the remainder in cash. The cash portion will be paid by cash in hand, a committed $1.0 billion senior secured term loan A facility and proceeds from a committed $700 million senior secured 364-day bridge loan facility. The transaction is expected to be closed by the current fiscal year ending January 31, 2026.

Currently, Dycom's exposure to data centers is primarily outside the walls (eg., long-haul fiber, interconnect work) but this acquisition brings the company inside the walls of the data centre (see image below).

This transaction is expected to be immediately accretive to adjusted EBITDA and EPS. Dycom is paying a 9.7x trailing four-quarter adjusted EBITDA multiple, and if future cash benefits from intangible asset tax amortization and goodwill are included, the multiple drops to roughly 8.5x. For a business growing revenue at about 15% with mid-to-high teens EBITDA margins, that seems like a very reasonable price.

Dycom itself trades at around 15.46x trailing EBITDA. So acquiring a company with better growth and profitability metrics at high single-digit multiples feels like a smart use of capital.

Strategically, this acquisition opens up a new market for the company. Power Solutions has a good presence in electrical contracting for data centers, with 90% of revenue tied to mission-critical data center projects. Until now, Dycom’s role in data centers has been mostly outside the walls, for example, long-haul fiber, campus builds, interconnect work, and so on. Power Solutions brings Dycom inside the walls of the data center, adding capabilities in electrical construction, power distribution, backup systems, and other advanced technical work. That positions Dycom more directly in the fast-growing data center sector.

Dycom already has exposure to the outside-plant data center network construction market, which is projected to be about $20 billion over the next five years. Power Solutions gives it exposure to a separate and much larger pool of spending, with management expecting roughly $240 billion of U.S. data-center-related labor spending over the same period.

There’s also a good cross-sell opportunity here. Dycom already works with hyperscalers on the fiber side, and Power Solutions has deep relationships with the same end users inside the data center. Putting both capabilities under one roof should open the door to broader project scopes, smoother coordination, and the chance to win work that spans both outside-plant and inside-the-facility components.

Financially, Power Solutions brings some structural advantages too. It’s less capital-intensive than Dycom’s telecom construction business and has a very fast cash conversion cycle, which means better free cash flow relative to revenue. The company also consistently runs at mid-to-high-teens EBITDA margins, and management expects that margin profile to continue. This should support Dycom’s consolidated margin trajectory over time.

The balance sheet also looks fine post-deal. Dycom expects pro forma net leverage to come in below 3x at closing, with management targeting a return to roughly 2x over the next 12 to 18 months. Overall, I believe it is a good deal that is reasonably priced, accretive, increases the addressable market, and has good cross-sell synergy potential.

Valuation

Since we’re already in the last quarter of the fiscal year, it makes more sense to look at the stock’s valuation on next year’s numbers. On FY27 estimates, Dycom trades at 25.59x consensus EPS of $12.71, which is a discount to its historical 5-year average forward P/E of 27.65x.

While the stock has seen almost ~10% jump post earnings, it is well supported by fundamentals, as the consensus EPS estimates have also been revised upwards by over 10% to account for Power Solution acquisition and good execution.

From a bigger-picture standpoint, valuation looks reasonable for a company with this level of visibility. The backlog is at record levels, the BEAD cycle is about to kick in, hyperscaler demand is inflecting, and FTTH deployment is poised to accelerate. All of that supports a long runway of healthy organic growth.

Margins also have room to grind higher as volume ramps, pricing stays firm, and the ERP rollout drives additional efficiencies. Layer in the Power Solutions deal bought at an attractive multiple with mid-to-high-teens EBITDA margins and strong free cash flow, the overall earnings trajectory looks even better. And importantly, leverage remains very manageable post-acquisition, with a path back toward 2x within 12–18 months.

Putting it all together, accelerating growth, improving margins, a well-priced acquisition, and a manageable balance sheet leave the stock looking fairly reasonably attractive for the kind of multi-year growth story that is lining up.

Risks

While I’m optimistic about the Power Solutions acquisition, it does introduce the usual integration and synergy-realization risks. The business Dycom is acquiring is high quality, but delivering on the cross-sell opportunity and scaling the platform the way management envisions will still require good execution.

On the demand side, hyperscalers and data centers look like powerful structural tailwinds. But it’s not risk-free. If AI adoption slows, or if hyperscale players pull back on planned capacity expansions, that would directly affect Dycom’s growth trajectory.

There are also risks if Dycom's customers bring more work in-house rather than contracting it to the company. I am not expecting it in the near term but it is a long-term risk.
